concept_name: When Two Projectiles Never Meet — The Miss Condition
source_book: DC Pandey 7.8 — two projectiles that miss; split from relative_motion_projectiles

real_world_anchor.primary: Two kids on a Jaipur rooftop during Uttarayan kite-festival. Each tosses a paper airplane toward the other — but neither aims precisely. One throws northward; the other throws at a 30-degree offset toward the east. In the other kid's frame, the incoming plane flies in a straight line that does NOT pass through him — it passes a few metres to his left. Result: they miss, the planes crash on the roof. Miss by any margin, and no amount of gravity will fix it.
real_world_anchor.secondary: Two Ultrabahubali fireworks shells on Republic Day at Rajpath, Delhi. Both lift at identical speeds but slightly different launch angles. From shell A's frame, shell B flies past at a constant offset. They miss by metres, bursting separately — as firework designers intended. To make them burst together, the launch geometry must be tuned so their relative straight-line trajectory passes through origin.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: An anti-ballistic missile test near Wheeler Island. Interceptor is aimed with a small angular error. In the target's frame, the interceptor moves in a straight line that narrowly misses — passing 50 metres to the side. A CEP (Circular Error Probable) measures exactly this 'how far off the straight-line relative trajectory passes' distance.

closest_approach_dist.formula: distance from origin to line through r_rel(0) along V_rel

miss_or_hit.formula: hit if closest_approach < epsilon, else miss

formulas.miss_condition: The relative trajectory is a straight line from r_rel(0) along V_rel.  They MISS unless this line passes through the origin.
formulas.closest_approach: d_miss = |r_rel(0) cross V_rel| / |V_rel|  (perpendicular distance from origin to the line)
formulas.contrast_with_collision: Collision is the special case d_miss = 0.

constraints: Both projectiles in free-fall under same gravity.
Miss distance is measured perpendicular to the relative velocity vector.
Real projectiles have finite size, so 'miss' usually means closest distance exceeds the projectile radius.

STATE_1.title: Same Magic, but Now They Miss

scene_composition.text:
Two projectiles, both in free-fall under gravity.

   Relative acceleration a_rel = 0  (as before).
   Relative motion is a STRAIGHT LINE.

   BUT the straight line does NOT pass through origin.

           r_rel(0) ___________
                   /          ~
                  /             ~
                 /               ~  <- straight-line relative path
                /                 ~
               /                   ~
              /                    ~
             O (origin = projectile 2)   ~~~~~~

   The CLOSEST APPROACH is the perpendicular distance
   from the origin to that straight line.

   If d_miss > 0, the two balls never collide.
   Gravity does not save them -- it cancels.
Jaipur rooftop Uttarayan paper airplane toss

tts_sentences.text_en: Same two-projectile setup — gravity still cancels in the relative frame, relative motion still a straight line.
But now the straight line does NOT pass through the origin.
The two projectiles pass each other by a perpendicular distance called closest approach — the miss distance.
If the miss distance is greater than zero, the balls never collide. Gravity cannot save them; it cancels out.

scene_composition.text:
Miss distance (perpendicular distance from origin to relative line):

   d_miss  =  |r_rel(0) x V_rel|  /  |V_rel|

  where x is the 2D vector cross-product (a scalar in 2D).

  Example: initial separation 30 m east, V_rel = (-5, 2) m/s.

     r_rel(0)  =  (-30, 0)    (points WEST from origin)
     V_rel     =  (-5, 2)

     cross   =  (-30) * (2)  -  (0) * (-5)
             =  -60

     |cross|  =  60
     |V_rel|  =  sqrt(25 + 4) = sqrt(29) ~= 5.39

     d_miss  =  60 / 5.39  ~= 11.13 m

  11 m apart at closest approach. They miss.

  Time of closest approach:
     t_min  =  -(r_rel(0) . V_rel) / |V_rel|^2
            =  -(-30*-5 + 0*2) / 29
            =  -150 / 29
            =  -5.17 s

  t_min is NEGATIVE, meaning closest approach is in the PAST.
  The projectiles are already moving APART. They will only
  get further away from now on.

tts_sentences.text_en: Miss distance is the perpendicular distance from the origin to the relative-motion line.
Formula: magnitude of r-rel-zero cross V-rel, divided by magnitude of V-rel.
For a 30-metre westward separation with relative velocity minus 5 comma plus 2, the miss distance works out to 11.13 metres.
You can also compute the TIME of closest approach. A negative time means the projectiles were closest in the past — they are currently moving apart, and the miss has already happened.

STATE_3.title: Aim Correction — How to Turn a Miss into a Hit

scene_composition.text:
To turn a miss into a hit:

   Current V_rel direction = some unit vector n_hat.
   Required V_rel direction = along (r_rel(0)) toward origin.

   Tilt your launch angle so that V_rel aligns with the
   line of sight between the two starting positions.

   One-line fix: aim the NEW projectile's velocity such that
      (V_1 - V_2) points exactly from r_rel(0) to origin.

   Common case: projectile 2 is stationary (V_2 = 0).
      Then V_rel = V_1, and projectile 1 must be aimed directly
      at projectile 2.  Classic monkey-hunter.

   This miss analysis is what anti-ballistic missile guidance
   exactly does in real-time: compute d_miss, correct V_rel,
   keep aiming, converge to zero.

tts_sentences.text_en: How do you turn a miss into a hit?
Tilt the launch angle until the relative velocity points exactly from r-rel-zero toward the origin.
In the common case where one projectile is stationary, that reduces to 'aim directly at the target' — the classic monkey-hunter rule.
This is literally what anti-ballistic missile guidance computes in real-time — measure the miss distance, correct the interceptor's velocity, iterate, converge to zero.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student believes that since gravity cancels in the relative frame, two projectiles launched in each other's direction will always eventually collide.
confusion_patterns: if gravity cancels projectiles must meet
relative frame zero acceleration means hit

scene_composition.text:
Student's error:
  "Since both feel the same gravity,
   the relative trajectory is a straight line.
   Any straight line between two points eventually
   hits something, right? So they must collide."

  Zero relative acceleration DOES NOT guarantee a hit.

  It only guarantees a STRAIGHT relative trajectory.

  A straight line can MISS the origin by any amount.

  Just like a bullet fired in the general direction of a target
  does not hit the target unless aimed precisely.

tts_sentences.text_en: Common error — student reads 'zero relative acceleration' and assumes automatic collision.
Zero relative acceleration only guarantees that the relative trajectory is a STRAIGHT LINE. It says nothing about whether that line passes through the origin.
Like a bullet fired in the general direction of a target, any straight line can still MISS by any amount if not aimed precisely.

scene_composition.text:
Picture this in projectile 2's frame:

    projectile 2  ->   O (at origin, stationary)
    projectile 1  ->   moves along a straight line
                       with velocity V_rel
                       starting from r_rel(0)

   If the line passes THROUGH origin:
     r_rel(t*) = 0 for some t*   ->  COLLIDE

   If the line passes NEAR origin but not through:
     r_rel never zero  ->  MISS with d_miss > 0

   If the line leads AWAY from origin:
     r_rel grows monotonically  ->  MISS and never come back

   Only the first case counts as a hit.  The other two are misses,
   no matter how long you wait.

tts_sentences.text_en: Three possible outcomes in the relative frame.
Line passes through origin — collision. Line passes near but not through — miss with positive distance. Line leads away from origin — miss that gets worse over time.
Only the first case is a hit. The other two are misses, no matter how long you wait.

scene_composition.text:
To guarantee a hit between two projectiles:

   Relative velocity V_rel MUST point exactly along
   the line connecting their starting positions.

   Geometrically: V_rel and r_rel(0) must be ANTI-parallel
   (pointing toward the origin from r_rel(0)).

   Any angular error produces a miss by
      d_miss = |r_rel(0)| * sin(angular_error).

   In JEE problems, aim correction is often just:
     'set the launch angle such that
      tan(theta_1) = dy / dx,
      where (dx, dy) = r_2(0) - r_1(0).'

   That means: launch projectile 1 aiming directly at
   projectile 2's starting point.

tts_sentences.text_en: Rule for a guaranteed hit — V-rel must point exactly from r-rel-zero toward the origin.
Any angular error of epsilon produces a miss distance of |r-rel-zero| times sine epsilon.

In JEE problems, the required aim is usually 'set launch angle such that tangent of theta equals delta-y over delta-x, where delta is the position difference'. In plain words — aim directly AT the target's starting position.

type_b.label: Two water balloons tossed at Holi
